plainMeaning: Picture a sacred eight-petalled lotus inside your body, resting on a channel that rises from the navel up to the crown of the head; the holy essence meditated upon there grants every good result.
recitation: nābhi-deśodbhavan-nāle, brahma-randhra-pratiṣṭhite | dhyātam aṣṭa-dale padme, tattvam etat phala-pradam ||

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit nābhi (f.) 'navel, hub, centre'; here the lower anchor-point of the inner channel.
wordByWord.notes: Forms the compound nābhi-deśa, 'the navel region', with the following token.

wordByWord.gloss: from the region (of the navel) — deśa 'region' + ud- 'up/out, arising'
wordByWord.etymology: deśa 'place, region' + ud- (verbal prefix, 'up, forth'); sandhi deśa + ud → deśod-, leading into udbhavat 'arising'.
wordByWord.notes: Book splits the long compound here; it continues into bhavat-nāle on the next token.

wordByWord.gloss: on the channel that arises (from there)
wordByWord.etymology: (ud)bhavat 'coming into being, arising' (pres. part. of √bhū) + nāla 'tube, stalk, channel' (locative nāle); -t + nā- → -nnā- by sandhi.
wordByWord.notes: Locative: 'on/in the nāla'. The nāla is the subtle inner conduit rising from the navel; cf. preface (nābhi-nāla).

wordByWord.etymology: brahman; in compound brahma-randhra it specifies the crown of the head.
wordByWord.notes: First member of brahma-randhra (glossed in preface).

wordByWord.gloss: aperture, opening — i.e. the brahma-randhra at the crown
wordByWord.etymology: randhra 'hole, opening, cleft'; brahma-randhra = 'aperture of brahman' at the skull's crown.
wordByWord.notes: Marks the upper anchor of the channel, opposite the navel.

wordByWord.etymology: past participle of prati-√sthā 'to stand firm, be established'; locative agreeing with nāle/padme — 'the [channel/lotus] that is set/rooted at the brahma-randhra'.
wordByWord.notes: Describes the channel-and-lotus as anchored at the crown-aperture.

wordByWord.etymology: dhyātam, past part. (n. sg.) of √dhyai 'to meditate'; aṣṭa(n) 'eight' (begins aṣṭa-dala). Joined by sandhi dhyātam + aṣṭa → dhyātamaṣṭa.
wordByWord.notes: dhyātam predicates the tattva ('this... is meditated'); aṣṭa belongs to the next compound aṣṭa-dale.

wordByWord.etymology: dala 'petal, leaf' (loc. dale); aṣṭa-dale 'eight-petalled', qualifying padme.
wordByWord.notes: With aṣṭa = aṣṭa-dala padma (eight-petalled lotus; preface).

wordByWord.etymology: padma 'lotus' (loc. padme); the inner lotus seat on which the tattva is visualized.
wordByWord.notes: Locus of the meditation: the eight-petalled inner lotus.

wordByWord.etymology: tattva 'that-ness, essence, principle' (preface); here the coloured mantra-essence held on the lotus.
wordByWord.notes: Subject of the sentence; final -m elided/joined into the next token.

wordByWord.etymology: carries the final -m of tattvam; etat 'this' (n. sg., demonstrative); phala 'fruit, result' (begins phala-pradam).
wordByWord.notes: Sandhi-cluster: tattvam etat phala = 'this tattva [is] fruit-...'.
Predicate adjective of tattvam etat: 'this tattva is fruit-bestowing'.

literalTranslation: On the channel arising from the navel region, [the channel/lotus] established at the brahma-randhra (crown-aperture) — meditated upon on the eight-petalled lotus — this tattva (essence) is fruit-bestowing.
idiomaticTranslation: Upon the subtle channel that rises out of the navel and stands rooted at the crown's brahma-randhra, set the eight-petalled lotus; the sacred essence meditated upon there is the bestower of every fruit.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse hands the practitioner the exact interior coordinates for the meditation the hymn has been building toward. Where the previous verse hurled the stambhana command outward to arrest 'approaching afflictions of karma', this verse turns wholly inward and quiet: it draws the vertical axis of the body — from nābhi-deśa (the navel region) up the rising nāla (subtle channel) to its rooting at the brahma-randhra (crown-aperture). On that axis it mounts the aṣṭa-dala padma, and on the lotus it seats the tattva to be 'meditated upon' (dhyātam). The four locatives — nāle, pratiṣṭhite, dale, padme — stack like a single address, so the reciter literally reads the verse as a map: channel → crown-anchor → lotus → petals. The whole sentence then resolves on one predicate: tattvam etat phala-pradam — 'this essence is fruit-bestowing.' Note the economy: the verse does not yet tell us the tattva's colour or syllables (that is held back for the next verse's four- and five-coloured sequence); here it only fixes the seat and asserts the result. The grammar is deliberately spare — bare locatives and a neuter participle — mirroring the stillness of the practice it prescribes. The verse thus pivots the hymn from protective command (vv.3–4) to contemplative ascent (vv.5–7), establishing the body itself as the ritual ground on which the mantra-king will, by v.8, 'awaken embodied in the heart.'
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse appropriates a tāntric/yogic body-map (navel-channel, crown-aperture, eight-petalled lotus) into a Jain devotional frame: the inner geography is not an end but the seat for dhyāna, and the promised phala is bound to the tattva — the mantra-essence of Pārśva — not to the chakra-mechanics themselves. This keeps the verse within Jain dhyāna, where right meditation (śubha/śukla-dhyāna) is itself the efficient cause of result, and the lotus is a focusing locus rather than a deity. The single neuter tattvam, left uncoloured here, signals that the 'reality' meditated is the supreme seed-mantra, deferred for chromatic specification to v.6.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter uses this verse as a guided visualization: settle the attention at the navel, draw a clear line of awareness up the central channel to the crown, rest there an imagined eight-petalled lotus, and hold the sacred essence (Pārśva's seed-mantra) steady upon it — trusting, per the verse's own promise, that this steadiness is itself phala-pradam, the giver of every wholesome result.

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭa-dala padma and the inner channel from navel to brahma-randhra (recurring imagery)
Following verse: the four- and five-coloured tattva meditated in sequence (v.6)
Piṇḍastha / padastha dhyāna with visualized lotus-seats

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Glosses and roots for nābhi, nāla, randhra, pratiṣṭhita (prati-√sthā), dhyāta (√dhyai), tattva, phala, pra-da (phala-pradam).

sources.title: Brahmarandhra
sources.consultedFor: Identification of the brahma-randhra as the crown-aperture and upper terminus of the inner meditative channel.

sources.title: Anahata (eight-petalled lotus / inner lotus symbolism)
sources.consultedFor: Background on the eight-petalled lotus (aṣṭa-dala padma) as a visualized inner seat in Indic meditation traditions.

sources.title: Parshvanatha
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Pārśvanātha, whose seed-mantra essence (tattva) is the object meditated on the lotus.

notes: The verse is Sanskrit (not Prakrit). Reconstructed sandhi: nābhi-deśa-udbhavat-nāle → nābhi-deśodbhavan-nāle; tattvam etat phala-pradam. The book splits the long first compound across the daṇḍa-line (દેશોદ્ | ભવન્નાલે) and joins dhyātam+aṣṭa and tattvam+etat by sandhi; tokenisation here follows the book's printed word-breaks, with sandhi members unpacked in each gloss. The locatives (nāle, pratiṣṭhite, dale, padme) are read as a single stacked description of one inner site; 'pratiṣṭhite' is taken to qualify the channel/lotus as rooted at the crown-aperture. The tattva's colour is intentionally unspecified here and is supplied by the following verse (v.6); no chromatic claim is imported into v.5. General yogic/tāntric parallels (Yogaśāstra) are offered as analogues only, not as the verse's source, per the tradition knowledge base's caution against importing non-attested ritual detail.
